We are taught to fear an uncontrolled tongue as one of the greatest evils. The affairs of people are thrown into disorder by what people say. Every period of history and every situation in life, whether private or public, provides examples of this. Hell has more influence on fueling a rash tongue than people generally think; and whenever people use their words for sin, they are set on fire from hell. No one can control the tongue without divine grace and help. The apostle does not say it is impossible, but that it is extremely difficult. Other sins lessen with age, but this often gets worse; we become more stubborn and irritable as our natural strength declines, and the days come when we have no joy. When other sins are tamed and subdued by the weaknesses of old age, the spirit often becomes sharper, nature being drawn down to the dregs, and the words used become more passionate. A person's tongue contradicts itself, which at one time claims to adore God’s perfections and to refer all things to him; and at another time condemns even good people, if they do not use the same words and expressions. True religion does not allow contradictions: how many sins would be prevented if people were always consistent! Pious and helpful language is the genuine product of a sanctified heart; and those who understand Christianity do not expect to hear curses, lies, boasting, or insults from a true believer’s mouth any more than they look for fruit from one tree from another. But facts show that more people succeed in controlling their senses and appetites than in properly restraining their tongues. Then, depending on divine grace, let us take care to bless and not curse; and let us aim to be consistent in our words and actions.

These verses show the difference between people pretending to be wise and actually being so. He who thinks well or he who speaks well is not wise in the sense of Scripture if he does not live and act well. True wisdom can be known by the gentleness of spirit and temper. Those who live in malice, envy, and strife live in confusion; and are likely to be provoked and hurried into any evil work. Such wisdom does not come down from above, but springs up from earthly principles, acts on earthly motives, and is intent on serving earthly purposes. The person who is elevated by such wisdom, as described by the apostle James, is close to Christian love, as described by the apostle Paul; and both are so described that every person may fully prove the reality of their achievements in them. It has no disguise or deceit. It cannot agree with those schemes the world considers wise, which are crafty and deceptive; but it is sincere, open, steady, uniform, and consistent with itself. May the purity, peace, gentleness, teachableness, and mercy shown in all our actions, and the fruits of righteousness abounding in our lives, prove that God has bestowed upon us this excellent gift.
